frozen_components: Ensemble and Random Forest authorship attribution classifiers (ENS, RFC)
architecture.summary: JamDec is a three-stage inference-time algorithm for authorship obfuscation that operates on small language models (GPT2-XL, 1.5B parameters). Stage 1 extracts content-preserving keywords using embedding-based (KeyBERT) and likelihood-based methods (GPT2, T5). Stage 2 generates multiple obfuscated candidates via Constrained Diverse Beam Search (CoDi-BS), which merges lexically constrained beam search with diversity scoring to produce varied rewrites that include extracted keywords. Stage 3 filters candidates using Natural Language Inference (entailment threshold) and Corpus of Linguistic Acceptability (grammaticality threshold) scores, optionally applying a stylometric obfuscator if no candidates pass filtering. The system preserves content by enforcing keyword inclusion and semantic entailment, while varying authorship style through generation diversity.

biblio.title: JAMDEC: Unsupervised Authorship Obfuscation using Constrained Decoding over Small Language Models

baseline.name: Mutant-X, Round-Trip Machine Translation, Stylometric, Paraphrasing

claims.statement: JamDec with GPT2-XL achieves the highest Task Score for almost every dataset, with Task Score on AMT-10 more than higher than almost all other methods on ENS and BertAA Task Score
JamDec (BertAA Task Score) is only lower than GPT3.5 175B on AMT-3

Using CoDi-BS leads to an overall increase in Drop Rate of and an increase in the number of sentences that pass the base NLI and CoLA threshold of about, with little change in NLI and CoLA score compared to only using CBS
JamDec strikes a better balance between content preservation and author obfuscation compared to task-specific baselines, lying closely to the top right in graphs of Drop Rate vs. NLI vs. CoLA

baseline.name: Mutant-X, Machine Translation, Round-Trip MT, Stylometric, Paraphrase

claims.statement: In human evaluation on AMT-3, JamDec performs better than all task-specific methods in Obfuscation by at least

baseline.name: GPT3.5 175B, Machine Translation, Paraphrase, Stylometric, Mutant-X

claims.statement: JamDec human evaluation scores are higher for Grammar and Fluency than most other methods, including GPT3.5, while performing on-par with GPT3.5 for Content Preservation

JamDec employs generations from a pre-trained language model which have been known to add factually incorrect or hallucinatory information, and despite content-preserving filters, additional information can bypass these filters and make it into the final obfuscation

JamDec may take a long time to employ if the approach is used at sentence-level on lengthy texts because it involves producing several candidates for each obfuscation, though it is slower than traditional stylometric-based methods
